zones.name: .rex
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 4 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These files are written by rex at runtime and consumed by AI tooling and dashboards; they are not source code but structural outputs
The prd.json and workflow.md serve as the source of truth for task orchestration and should be treated as append-safe, atomic-write targets
execution-log.jsonl enables audit and replay; consider whether log rotation policy is documented or enforced
Cohesion and coupling are both 0.0 — expected for a data-only zone with no import edges. This zone is correctly isolated from source code.
prd.json is the only file with concurrent-write risk (noted in CLAUDE.md); no locking mechanism exists, so consumers should treat it as eventually consistent under concurrent plan/work runs.
workflow.md is human-readable state documentation — keeping it co-located with the machine-readable prd.json is a good discoverability pattern.

The absence of any write-locking mechanism for prd.json is not just a concurrency documentation gap — it is an implementation gap: sync-engine.ts and sync.ts in analyze-engine write to this file, and no atomic-write protocol (temp-file + rename, advisory lock, or write journal) is enforced at the call site. The CLAUDE.md warning exists at the operator level but nothing prevents a library consumer from writing incorrectly.

prd.json has no enforced atomic-write contract at the implementation level. sync-engine.ts and sync.ts write to it directly with no temp-file-and-rename or advisory lock, making the concurrent-write risk documented in CLAUDE.md a latent data-corruption bug rather than an operator caution.

execution-log.jsonl has no documented or enforced size cap — in long-running projects with frequent hench runs, the file will grow unbounded. Unlike prd.json (which is rewritten) or workflow.md (which is overwritten), jsonl files only accumulate lines, making unbounded growth the default behavior with no trim mechanism visible in the codebase.

workflow.md is a human-readable artifact while config.json, prd.json, and execution-log.jsonl are all machine-readable. Co-locating mixed-audience artifacts in the same zone conflates documentation state with operational state — workflow.md serves a different reader (human operators) than its zone siblings (AI tooling, dashboards).
execution-log.jsonl has no size cap or rotation mechanism. Add a trim-to-N-lines step after each hench run write to prevent unbounded growth in long-lived projects.

workflow.md (human-readable) is co-located with machine-readable operational files. Consider whether it belongs in a separate documentation artifact or if its regeneration contract should be explicitly documented alongside prd.json.

mcp-tools.ts mixes static and dynamic import of the same module: TOOL_VERSION is statically imported at the top while REX_DIR is lazily loaded via await import() inside a function body — inconsistent import strategies within a single file are a reliable indicator of an underlying circular dependency that was patched with a dynamic import workaround rather than resolved structurally

TOOL_VERSION and REX_DIR both reside in src/cli/commands/constants.ts — a CLI command file — yet TOOL_VERSION is consumed by MCP service infrastructure (mcp.ts, mcp-tools.ts) and REX_DIR by 20+ files across all zones; these two constants serve entirely different purposes and their co-location in CLI command infrastructure creates artificial cross-layer coupling

mcp-tools.ts uses a dynamic await import('./commands/constants.js') for REX_DIR inside a function body while statically importing TOOL_VERSION from the same file at the module top-level. This inconsistency indicates a circular dependency that was patched with a lazy import rather than resolved by extracting shared constants to a foundation module. The static import path and dynamic import path must be reconciled.

mcp-tools.ts imports 20+ items directly from core domain modules (tree, stats, delete, next-task, transitions, timestamps, parent-completion, dag, cascade-reset, move, merge, verify, reorganize, reorganize-executor, health, facets) with no gateway/facade layer. Every other cross-package consumer in the monorepo uses a gateway; the absence of one here makes mcp-tools.ts the highest-risk refactoring surface in the package.

REX_DIR is defined in src/cli/commands/constants.ts and imported by 20+ files spanning every zone in the codebase. A path constant that is truly package-wide should live in a package-level constants module (e.g. src/constants.ts), not inside CLI command infrastructure. Every non-CLI zone that imports REX_DIR is forced to depend on CLI command internals solely to read a directory path string.

The dynamic imports in mcp-tools.ts serve two structurally distinct purposes that should not be conflated: (1) legitimate lazy-loading of optional LLM features (reshape-reason.js, reason.js, project-config.js — loaded only when mode !== 'fast') and (2) a cycle-break workaround for REX_DIR from constants.js. Only the second is a structural problem; the first is intentional performance optimization.

mcp.ts is classified with archetype 'cli-command' but it is a transport-agnostic server factory exporting createRexMcpServer() and startMcpServer(). The 'service' or 'entrypoint' archetype more accurately describes its role — the current archetype label will mislead future zone classification.

workflow/default.ts contains a single exported string constant (10-step agent workflow instructions for hench) with zero runtime relationship to MCP transport or CLI commands. Its placement in this zone is purely positional proximity in the file system, not semantic.

TOOL_VERSION is hardcoded as '0.1.0' in constants.ts independently of package.json. Version drift between the CLI help text and the published npm package version is undetectable at build time and will silently diverge as the package is released.

mcp.ts is classified as archetype 'cli-command' but exports a transport-agnostic server factory (createRexMcpServer). Reclassify it as 'service' to accurately reflect its role and prevent future files being co-located based on a misleading archetype label.

workflow/default.ts (a single string constant with no imports) has no semantic relationship to MCP bootstrap infrastructure. Relocate it to src/workflow/ as a standalone constant or to a domain-config module to remove it from the CLI bootstrap zone and improve zone cohesion.

Audit each of the 13 e2e test files for duplicated setup patterns (process spawning, temp directory creation, fixture loading). The cli zone (11 files, cohesion 0.22) is imported by BOTH cli-command-layer (12 edges) AND analyze-engine (10 edges) — a poorly-cohesed foundation zone serving as a shared dependency for two of the three highest-traffic zones compounds structural fragility for both consumers simultaneously.

unit-analyze imports 26 items from unit-cli (cli-command-layer) and 10 from the mcp-cli-bootstrap zone — the domain engine depending on CLI command and bootstrap infrastructure is a layering inversion; these return imports are the primary root cause of all bidirectional coupling warnings in the codebase

The three-way cycle unit-analyze ↔ unit ↔ unit-cli (74 + 69 + 21 = 164 total edges) forms a fully-connected circular triad that functions as a single merged module at runtime — no meaningful layer boundary exists between these three zones, making independent versioning, testing, or extraction of any one impossible without touching all three

prd-validation, task-selection-engine, and prd-fix-command each maintain near-unidirectional dependencies on unit-analyze — these three zones collectively demonstrate that the correct consumer pattern is achievable and only the three-way unit/unit-analyze/unit-cli triad is structurally broken

The unit-analyze → store (14 imports) return flow identifies a specific abstraction leak: the analyze engine likely holds a reference to a store instance or calls adapter methods directly rather than accepting results through injection, coupling domain logic to persistence infrastructure

A clear two-tier consumer split exists: zones with near-clean unidirectional imports (validate, task-selection, fix) vs zones forming circular triads (unit-analyze, unit, unit-cli) — prioritizing the triad dissolution over the high-coupling unidirectional zones would have the highest architectural ROI

The mcp-cli-bootstrap zone receives 28 imports from unit-analyze and sends 10 back, but also receives 12 imports from unit-cli — it sits at the junction of all three triadic zones, making it the most likely location where shared utility code that belongs in a foundation module has been left in-place

The two zones with perfect cohesion (notion-store-client and e2e-tests) both have zero external coupling — this correlation across two independent data points suggests that coupling actively suppresses cohesion in this codebase, not just by Louvain algorithm artifact but as a structural causal relationship.

validate and unit-core are the only zones that consume unit-analyze unidirectionally — their shared characteristic is a tightly-scoped single concern (validation and task selection respectively). This confirms that concern scope, not just import count, determines whether a zone escapes the circular dependency field around unit-analyze.

The codebase has three distinct coupling tiers: (1) zero-coupling leaf zones (notion-store-client, e2e-tests), (2) clean unidirectional consumers (validate, unit-core), and (3) circular dependency participants (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, cli, store). There is no middle tier — zones either escape the cycle entirely or are fully entangled, suggesting the circular dependency system is self-reinforcing and will require deliberate architectural intervention to break.

The cli zone's structural position as a potential mediator between unit-cli and unit-analyze (with 12 and 28 import edges respectively) suggests the original design intent was a layered architecture, but unit-analyze's 26 reverse imports into unit-cli indicate the layer boundary collapsed during feature development — the architectural intent is recoverable if unit-analyze's outbound imports to unit-cli are extracted into a shared interface.

The three-zone coupling triangle (analyze-engine, cli-command-layer, prd-graph-utilities) has all three pairs independently bidirectional with no unidirectional edge in any pair — this is a fully-entangled dependency cluster, not a directed triangle. No single zone can be extracted without touching code in all three.

Fan-in asymmetry is extreme: analyze-engine has 6 direct cross-zone consumers with no facade, while unit-store, e2e, and fix have zero inbound edges. The dependency graph has no middle tier — zones are either pure hubs or pure leaves with nothing in between.

Zones with high inbound fan-in consistently have lower cohesion (analyze-engine: 154 inbound, cohesion 0.68; cli-command-layer: 26 inbound, cohesion 0.46; prd-graph-utilities: 43 inbound, cohesion 0.28). Zones with zero inbound have cohesion ≥ 1.0. Fan-in is a reliable proxy for cohesion risk and can guide prioritization of refactoring targets without requiring cohesion measurement.

unit-analyze imports from three of its consumer zones (unit-cli: 26, store: 14, mcp-cli-bootstrap: 10) — the domain engine is not truly authoritative; it is entangled with CLI, adapter, and bootstrap layers. Extracting a shared-types foundation module that all zones import unidirectionally would sever all three of these inversions simultaneously.

The codebase has two architectural tiers of zone health: clean consumers (validate, task-selection, fix — near-unidirectional) and circular participants (unit-analyze, unit, unit-cli — triadic cycle). Refactoring effort should be directed exclusively at the triadic participants; the clean consumers require only coupling-count reduction, not structural repair.

No zone in the codebase imports from both validate and task-selection-engine simultaneously — these two zones are effectively isolated from each other despite sharing the same upstream (unit-analyze). This orthogonality is a positive signal that the command/concern separation at the CLI level is working correctly.

All three circular dependency cycles (unit↔unit-analyze, unit-cli↔unit-analyze, cli↔unit-analyze via unit-cli) share unit-analyze as a common node — the analyze-engine is both the domain authority and the primary source of coupling instability. Defining a stable facade at the unit-analyze boundary would simultaneously reduce all three cycles.

The codebase exhibits a coupling polarization: zones are either perfectly isolated (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) or deeply entangled in the circular dependency cluster. No zone sits at an intermediate healthy coupling level (e.g., 0.1–0.3), indicating the current zone boundaries do not reflect the natural modularity of the codebase.

validate and unit-core provide concrete existence proofs that clean unidirectional consumption of unit-analyze is achievable — their import patterns should be used as reference contracts when extracting interfaces from the circular dependency zones.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts being imported by 7 zones functions as an implicit global configuration layer with no indirection. A rename or semantic change to any exported constant cascades to all 7 zones simultaneously. A dedicated constants gateway or enums module with a controlled re-export surface would provide the missing indirection layer.

The coupling polarization (zones are either cohesion 1.0 / coupling 0.0 or fully entangled in the circular triad) means new features have no healthy intermediate-coupling zone to attach to — they are forced to either create an isolated island or join the circular dependency cluster. The absence of a middle tier is self-reinforcing: every new feature either escapes completely or gets absorbed.

The fix zone (4 files, cohesion 0.25, zero cross-zone edges in either direction) is undetectable as live or dead code from the import graph alone. If it is live, it is an undiscovered island; if it is dead, it is accumulated waste. A deliberate audit is required because the structural metrics cannot distinguish between the two cases.

unit-store (4 files, Notion adapter, cohesion 1.0) and store/remote-sync-adapters share a domain concern (PRD sync to external services) but are structurally isolated zones with no import edges between them. If they share domain types or interface contracts through a third zone (analyze-engine), the apparent isolation is illusory — actual type coupling is hidden behind indirect imports.

src/analyze/guided.ts (domain analyze layer, unit-analyze zone) imports the `info` function from src/cli/output.ts (CLI presentation layer) — this is the most concrete single-file instance of the analyze-engine's layering inversion. A domain module that constructs LLM prompts or orchestrates analysis should never import from CLI output utilities; the output call should be lifted to the CLI command layer that invokes guided.ts.

The REX_DIR path constant functions as a de-facto package-level constant but is defined inside CLI command infrastructure (src/cli/commands/constants.ts), forcing all 20+ consumers across domain, store, and MCP zones to take a dependency on CLI command internals. Promoting REX_DIR to a top-level src/constants.ts would remove the single largest source of artificial cross-layer coupling in the import graph without any behavioral change.

The dynamic import workaround in mcp-tools.ts (await import('./commands/constants.js') for REX_DIR while statically importing TOOL_VERSION from the same file) is a concrete, locatable artifact of the circular dependency problem identified in prior passes. It is the only dynamic import in the package used as a cycle-break and should be treated as a TODO marker for the structural fix rather than an acceptable long-term pattern.

src/cli/output.ts is imported by 27 files yet only imports from @n-dx/llm-client — it is functionally a foundation utility that has been misclassified as CLI infrastructure. Its broad fan-in across domain and presentation zones while having zero internal dependencies qualifies it as a candidate for promotion to a shared utilities layer, which would also resolve the analyze/guided.ts layering inversion.

The cohesion distribution across zones is bimodal: values cluster at 0–0.28 (structurally broken) and 0.36–1.0 (structurally sound), with no zones in the 0.29–0.35 range. Bimodal cohesion distributions indicate two distinct module design regimes coexisting in the codebase — files are either in the circular dependency field (low cohesion) or outside it (higher cohesion). There is no gradual degradation path; zones enter or exit the cycle as a unit.

The import density between unit-cli and unit-analyze (43 imports across 37 source files = 1.16 imports/file) exceeds 1.0, meaning the Louvain zone boundary has zero practical enforcement value for these two zones. Import density > 1.0 between two zones is a reliable threshold indicator that the zones should be merged or that a shared foundation extraction is the only viable remediation.

No zone in the codebase has both non-trivial coupling (> 0.1) AND high cohesion (> 0.8) simultaneously — the correlation between coupling and low cohesion is consistent across all 12 zones. This confirms that coupling is causally suppressing cohesion in this codebase, not merely correlated with it, and that coupling reduction must precede cohesion improvement in any refactoring sequence.

Integration test files are distributed into production zones (e.g. smart-add integration tests in the cli bootstrap zone) rather than residing in a dedicated integration tier. This contamination artificially inflates production zone file counts, reduces their cohesion scores, and obscures the true test coverage topology. A convention enforcing that all integration tests live in a dedicated zone would improve cohesion metrics across multiple production zones without changing production code.

The codebase has a complete absence of intermediate coupling zones (coupling between 0.1 and 0.5 with non-zero inbound edges): zones are either pure hubs with many consumers or pure leaves with zero consumers. The missing middle tier — stable domain modules with 1–3 consumers — is the architectural gap that the foundation module extraction should fill. Until intermediate-coupling zones exist, the hub-or-leaf polarization will reassert itself after any refactor.

src/cli/commands/constants.ts is a de-facto global constants layer imported by 7 zones with no gateway or re-export indirection. Any change to an exported constant requires searching and updating all 7 consuming zones simultaneously, with no type boundary to contain the blast radius.

The coupling polarization (zones are either perfectly isolated or fully entangled with no intermediate tier) means the architecture has no healthy intermediate attachment point for new features. New code will default to joining the circular dependency cluster rather than forming clean unidirectional relationships, making the structural debt self-reinforcing.

token counting logic is split between cli-command-layer (usage/format display) and the hench package (actual agent token tracking) with no shared contract or type boundary. Silent divergence between reported and actual token consumption is possible and currently undetectable by the type system or test suite.

src/analyze/guided.ts (analyze-engine zone) imports `info` from src/cli/output.ts (CLI presentation zone), inverting the domain→presentation dependency direction. This is a concrete, fixable instance of the broader unit-analyze→unit-cli reverse coupling: moving the `info()` call to the CLI command layer that invokes guided.ts would sever this specific inversion without any architectural restructuring.

REX_DIR is defined in CLI command infrastructure (src/cli/commands/constants.ts) but consumed by 20+ files spanning every architectural zone including domain, store, and MCP layers. This forces domain-layer modules to import from CLI command internals to obtain a simple path string. REX_DIR should be extracted to a package-level constants file (src/constants.ts or src/package-constants.ts) with no zone affiliation.

src/cli/output.ts has 27 inbound imports spanning every zone (domain, store, MCP, CLI command) yet has zero internal dependencies beyond @n-dx/llm-client. A presentation utility with this fan-in profile is functioning as a foundation module but is located in CLI infrastructure — its physical location in src/cli/ causes every domain importer to cross a layer boundary. Relocating it to src/util/output.ts or promoting it to a shared layer would normalize the import graph without code changes.

No stable public facade exists on the unit-analyze (analyze-engine) zone despite it being imported by 6 consumer zones across 3 coupling tiers. All 6 consumers reach directly into internal module paths, meaning unit-analyze has no enforced API surface. Defining a single re-export facade file as the only permitted import entry point for external zones is the highest-ROI structural change available — it would reduce the blast radius of internal reorganization from 6 zones to 0 without changing any logic.

The three-zone circular triad (unit-analyze ↔ unit ↔ unit-cli) has import density exceeding 1.0 on two of its three edges (unit-cli→unit-analyze: 43/37, unit-analyze→unit-cli: 26/37). At this density, the zones cannot be independently tested, versioned, or extracted — they must be treated as a single module for all practical purposes. Acknowledging this in architecture documentation and treating the triad as a single refactoring unit (rather than three separate improvements) would prevent partial fixes that leave the cycle intact.

Integration test files are co-located with production source zones (smart-add integration tests appear in the cli bootstrap zone) instead of a dedicated integration test zone. This contamination lowers cohesion scores for production zones, inflates their file counts, and makes it impossible to run test tiers independently by zone. A project-wide convention requiring test files to reside in zones named with a '-tests' or '-test' suffix would resolve this across all affected zones.

The three triadic zones (analyze-engine, cli-command-layer, prd-graph-utilities) all have mismatched internal IDs and display names (unit-analyze/analyze-engine, unit-cli/cli-command-layer, unit/prd-graph-utilities). This dual naming system is consistent within the triad — suggesting the abbreviated IDs are legacy names predating a display-name update that was never propagated to the import tracking data layer. No zone outside the triad has this mismatch, confirming it is a triad-specific artifact.

The fix zone has cohesion 0.25 AND zero cross-zone coupling — the only zone in the codebase with both low cohesion and zero coupling simultaneously. Unit-store and e2e achieve high cohesion with zero coupling because their files are tightly related. Fix zone files are not tightly related to each other (cohesion 0.25) but also import from nowhere and are imported by nowhere. This profile is consistent with four independently dead or entry-point-only files grouped together by the community detection residual rather than by actual domain affiliation — the zone may not represent a real architectural unit at all.

No zone naming convention is documented or enforced. Zone names mix architectural tier labels (-layer suffix in cli-command-layer), domain compound nouns (analyze-engine, prd-graph-utilities), runtime state descriptors (rex-runtime-state), and abbreviated legacy IDs (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli). The lack of a naming schema means each new zone will be named ad hoc, making the zone namespace progressively harder to navigate as the codebase grows.

The three-way ID/display-name mismatch across the triadic zones means any automated tool that joins zone health metrics (keyed by display name) with cross-zone import counts (keyed by internal ID) will silently produce incorrect results unless the translation is hardcoded. This is a data-integrity gap in the sourcevision output format — zone IDs should be stable, unique, and consistent across all output artifacts.

Binary assets (Rex.png, Rex-F.png) and AI-generated text artifacts (llms.txt, context.md) are currently processed by the Louvain algorithm as ordinary files. Binary files produce zero import edges by definition and will always be assigned to the lowest-density residual zone. Excluding binary files and generated AI artifacts from zone analysis before running Louvain would improve the accuracy of community detection for all zones by removing guaranteed noise nodes from the graph.

src/cli/output.ts is a layered extension adapter over @n-dx/llm-client: it re-exports llm-client primitives (info, result, setQuiet, isQuiet) and adds rex-specific utilities (warn with deprecation semantics, startSpinner with TTY awareness). It cannot be trivially promoted into @n-dx/llm-client because the additions are rex domain concerns. The correct promotion path is src/util/output.ts within the rex package, which would resolve the layering inversion from analyze/guided.ts without requiring changes to @n-dx/llm-client.

The 5 integration tests in tests/integration/ cover four distinct concerns: store roundtrips (store-roundtrip.test.ts), LLM config persistence (claude-config.test.ts, project-config.test.ts), and smart-add behaviors (duplicate-outcomes, orphaned-parent). Only the smart-add tests are surfaced in zone metadata; the store and config integration tests are invisible to zone-based analysis. Zone tooling that excludes test files from zone membership will systematically undercount integration coverage for the store and config zones.

The schema barrel (src/schema/index.ts) and store facade (src/store/index.ts) share an implicit convention: neither exports Zod validation schemas, forcing consumers to import src/schema/validate.js directly. This protects consumers from a forced Zod runtime dependency but is undocumented. A contributor adding a new PRD type will likely add its Zod schema to index.ts by analogy with the type exports, unintentionally breaking the convention and forcing Zod on all schema consumers.

The store facade (src/store/index.ts, 41 exports, gateway-like indirection) and the absence of any equivalent facade on src/analyze or unit-analyze creates an asymmetry in refactoring risk: store consumers are insulated from internal restructuring, while unit-analyze consumers (6 zones, 154 inbound edges) absorb every internal path change directly. Adding a single re-export facade at the unit-analyze boundary is the highest-ROI single-file change available in the entire codebase.

The one-test-file-per-module naming convention (core/next-task.ts → tests/unit/core/next-task.test.ts) is followed uniformly across src/core/ except for next-task.ts itself, which has 5 test files. This exception is undocumented. Documenting the threshold for when a module earns specialized suites (e.g., 'algorithms with multiple independent scoring dimensions') would prevent the convention from appearing violated to new contributors inspecting the test directory.

Both zero-coupling zones (notion-store-client and e2e-tests) achieve their isolation through fundamentally different mechanisms: notion-store-client is isolated because it is a leaf-node adapter with no downstream consumers, while e2e-tests is isolated because black-box CLI tests by definition cannot import production source. Conflating these two isolation types risks treating e2e isolation as a design achievement rather than a structural property of the test tier.

The codebase has no 'integration-tests' zone despite having smart-add integration tests co-located in the cli bootstrap zone and a store-contract test in the notion-store-client zone — integration test files are scattered across at least two production zones, making the integration tier invisible as a structural entity. A dedicated integration-tests zone (parallel to e2e-tests) would surface the integration coverage gap and separate it from production source file counts.

The two exemplar zones (notion-store-client, e2e-tests) and the clean unidirectional consumers (validate, unit-core) share a common structural property: each has a single, clearly scoped concern. The circular dependency participants (unit-analyze, unit, unit-cli) each span multiple concerns. Concern scope — not import count or zone size — is the leading indicator of whether a zone will achieve or maintain healthy cohesion; this should be the primary criterion when planning new zone boundaries.

notion-store-client's store-contract.test.ts and e2e-tests' zero-coupling property together reveal a testing architecture gap: the adapter contract is validated at unit granularity (store-contract.test.ts tests the Notion implementation in isolation) but never validated at the CLI boundary level (e2e-tests has no Notion-specific test file). A full-stack adapter regression would be undetectable by either test tier independently.

The codebase has no zone named with a 'integration' suffix despite having integration-tier test files distributed across production zones. The '-tests' suffix convention established by e2e-tests and implicitly by notion-store-client's co-located tests is inconsistently applied — unit tests appear in every production zone without the suffix, while only the black-box e2e tier gets its own named zone. Standardizing on a zone-per-test-tier naming convention ('unit-tests', 'integration-tests', 'e2e-tests') would make the three-tier test pyramid structurally visible in the zone graph.

Document a zone naming convention in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md specifying: (1) zone IDs and display names must be identical, (2) no architectural tier suffixes, (3) names should reflect domain concern not implementation pattern. Apply retroactively to the three triadic zones and cli-command-layer.

Publish a canonical zone-id mapping artifact (e.g. a zones.json with id, displayName, primaryDirectory, fileCount) so that import metrics and health metrics can be joined by machines and humans alike without a mental translation step.

Exclude binary assets (*.png, *.jpg, *.svg) and generated AI artifacts (llms.txt, context.md as identified by generation headers) from the Louvain community detection input graph. These files produce zero import edges and degrade zone cohesion scores for the zones they are assigned to.

Audit the fix zone to determine whether its 4 files are live code reachable from any CLI entry point. If unreachable, delete them. If live, document why they have zero import edges — they may be dynamically required or invoked via CLI spawn rather than imported.

The Zod-exclusion convention in src/schema/index.ts and src/store/index.ts (types exported, Zod schemas not) is undocumented. Add a comment block at the top of both index files explaining the separation rationale and pointing to validate.js as the correct import for validation schemas. Without documentation, new contributors will violate the convention by analogy.

src/cli/output.ts is a layered extension of @n-dx/llm-client with rex-specific additions (warn, startSpinner). Relocate it from src/cli/output.ts to src/util/output.ts to signal its cross-zone foundation role and resolve the layering inversion where analyze/guided.ts imports from CLI infrastructure.

Adding a single re-export facade file (e.g., src/analyze/index.ts) as the sole permitted external import entry point for the unit-analyze zone would reduce blast radius of internal reorganization from 6 consumer zones to 0, without changing any logic. This is the highest-ROI structural change available — no behavior changes, no refactoring of consumers required if the facade maps existing internal paths.

Establish a formal zone naming convention document distinguishing three categories: (1) production zones using domain-role names (analyze-engine, remote-sync-adapters), (2) test zones using '<tier>-tests' suffix (e2e-tests, integration-tests), and (3) adapter zones using '<vendor>-adapter' pattern. The current codebase mixes all three without a documented standard, making zone boundary decisions inconsistent for new contributors.

The store-contract.test.ts in notion-store-client validates a cross-zone interface contract that the import graph cannot represent. Introduce a ZONE_CONTRACTS.md or inline comment convention to document semantic cross-zone dependencies (interface definitions tested in a different zone) so they survive import graph refactors and zone merges without silent contract drift.

Integration test files are currently distributed into production zones (smart-add in cli bootstrap, store-contract in notion-store-client), making the integration test tier invisible as a structural entity. Extract all integration tests into a dedicated 'integration-tests' zone to complete the three-tier pyramid (unit-tests, integration-tests, e2e-tests) visible in the zone graph, separate from production source file counts.

Systemic documentation debt: findings 7, 8, 12, and 13 collectively reveal that multiple architectural conventions (zone naming, Zod-exclusion, cross-zone contracts) exist in practice but are entirely undocumented. This is not four separate issues — it is one systemic failure to document conventions as they are established, compounding over time.

CLI boundary erosion is a recurring independent signal: findings 4, 14, 17, and 19 each independently identify src/cli/ as having unclear or violated boundaries — 8 zones colocated in src/cli/commands/, a utility module causing a layering inversion into analyze/, and tight 57-call unidirectional coupling between output.ts and prune.ts. Four independent findings on the same layer is the strongest zone-level signal in the dataset.

Test zone over-segmentation: five unit-tier zones (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-store, unit-core) exist with no equivalent segmentation in production zones. This asymmetry inflates apparent zone count and may make coupling metrics for test zones misleading, since test coupling to implementation is expected and not a structural risk.

The bidirectional coupling between analyze-engine and prd-graph-utilities (finding 0: 39+35 crossings) combined with both appearing in finding 16's high-risk list and finding 4's directory co-location creates a three-signal convergence on the PRD graph/task domain as a structural knot — making it the concrete target for finding 5's facade extraction, not a vague structural area.

Finding 2's size imbalance comparison (105 files vs 4 files) is misleading and should not drive refactoring decisions. The 105-file zone is a test zone (unit-analyze) whose size mirrors the production code under test — this is expected and healthy. The 4-file zone is the fix zone, whose concern is isolation not size. Comparing them as evidence of structural imbalance conflates test-tier and production-tier zone counts.
src/cli/commands/ contains files from 8 zones (cli, fix, store, unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core, validate) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Findings 14 and 17 directly contradict each other on the correct destination for src/cli/output.ts. Finding 17 (automated heuristic) recommends moving it into src/cli/commands/ based on 89% import-neighbor proximity. Finding 14 (LLM analysis) recommends moving it to src/util/output.ts to resolve a layering inversion where analyze/guided.ts imports CLI infrastructure. Finding 17's destination is architecturally incorrect: proximity-based relocation would deepen the CLI identity of the file, worsening the layering inversion rather than fixing it. Finding 14's destination (src/util/output.ts) is the valid recommendation; finding 17 correctly identifies the misplacement but prescribes the wrong fix.

The fan-in hotspot at src/schema/index.ts (finding 3: 24 importer files, 8 zones) combined with the undocumented Zod-exclusion convention (finding 12) creates a compound risk: the highest-impact module in the codebase has an invisible behavioral contract. A contributor adding a Zod schema export to schema/index.ts would silently violate the convention with no static check, no lint rule, and no comment to warn them. Adding a comment block to schema/index.ts itself — not just PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md — is higher priority because the fan-in multiplier means violations spread to 24 files instantly.

Findings 7 and 8 address the same problem (zone naming convention) at different levels of specificity. Finding 7 specifies three concrete rules; finding 8 describes a three-category taxonomy. These are complementary, not redundant: finding 7 addresses IDs and display names (the naming syntax), finding 8 addresses semantic categories (the naming vocabulary). A single PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md section can satisfy both by defining the taxonomy first, then the syntax rules that apply within each category.

Systemic root cause: the high-coupling/low-cohesion pattern appearing independently in cli, unit, unit-core, fix, store, and validate is not six separate problems — it is one structural problem. unit-analyze's public API is too broad and too accessible, drawing all neighboring zones into strong dependency relationships rather than mediating through a narrow interface. Zone-by-zone refactoring will not resolve this; only stabilizing unit-analyze's public boundary will.

Directory drift as architectural signal: five zones have files spread across 5–14 directories (unit: 14, unit-analyze: 13, cli: 8, store: 7, unit-cli: 6). Zone boundaries exist in the import graph but not on disk, making them invisible to contributors navigating the file tree. This drift explains why the high-coupling pattern persists — there is no directory-level friction discouraging casual cross-zone imports.

Small-zone metric noise: zones with fewer than 8 files (.rex: 4, fix: 4, unit-store: 4) produce coupling and cohesion ratios that are mathematically valid but statistically unreliable. A single import decision moves the needle by 0.25 or more. Automated severity ratings for these zones should be evaluated against qualitative criteria (are responsibilities well-defined?) rather than treated as equivalent evidence to the same ratios in 30+ file zones.

The bidirectional coupling between unit and unit-analyze (35+39 crossings) is the single highest-priority structural issue by crossing count. However, both directions of the coupling need separate diagnosis: unit → unit-analyze crossings likely represent legitimate consumption of shared core; unit-analyze → unit crossings (39) represent the riskier direction where the dependency flows upward into a consumer zone and should be examined for what unit-analyze is importing back from unit.

Add a comment block to src/schema/index.ts documenting the Zod-exclusion convention. The compound risk identified in global finding 11 — fan-in hotspot (24 importer files, 8 zones) combined with undocumented behavioral contract — means a single silent violation propagates to the widest-impact module in the codebase instantly. A file-level comment is higher-priority than PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md documentation because contributors encounter it at the point of change, not during onboarding.

The six zones flagged as high-risk (cli, unit, unit-core, fix, store, validate) share a single distinguishing characteristic: each has its primary coupling target as unit-analyze, not internal zone cohesion. The star-coupling topology — one zone absorbing imports from all peers — is the systemic anti-pattern. Addressing individual zone cohesion scores without narrowing unit-analyze's public API will not break the pattern.

The automated heuristic severity ratings are internally consistent but systematically inflate risk for small zones (fix, unit-store, .rex). The catastrophic/critical thresholds were calibrated on zone populations where metric values represent meaningful structural signals. For zones under 8 files, the same threshold values represent noise. This is not a flaw in individual findings but a calibration gap that affects how the summary in global finding 8 (6 zones exceed thresholds) should be weighted: the 4-file zones should be excluded from that count as unreliable data points.

Star-coupling topology is the dominant systemic anti-pattern: unit-analyze is the hub absorbing imports from cli (28), unit (35), unit-cli (43), fix (3), unit-core (9), store (19), validate (20). Addressing individual zone cohesion scores without narrowing unit-analyze's public API surface will not break this pattern — the topology will reassert itself as zones evolve.

Small zone metric inflation affects fix (4 files), unit-store (4 files), .rex (4 files), and unit-core (7 files). The catastrophic/critical thresholds were calibrated on zones with meaningful import populations. For zones under 8 files, a single cross-zone import produces metric values indistinguishable from structural failure. These four zones should be excluded from architectural risk counts.

The bidirectional coupling between unit and unit-analyze (35 + 39 crossings) has two qualitatively different halves: unit → unit-analyze is expected consumer-to-core flow; unit-analyze → unit is a dependency inversion where the shared core imports from a consumer. Only the 39-crossing upward direction requires immediate remediation.

The move-file contradiction between global findings 8 and 11 (src/cli/output.ts) is resolved by finding 8: proximity-based relocation to src/cli/commands/ would deepen the layering inversion; the correct destination is src/util/output.ts to resolve the inversion where analyze/guided.ts imports CLI infrastructure. Finding 11's diagnosis of misplacement is correct; its prescribed destination is wrong.

The six zones flagged as high-risk (cli, unit, unit-core, fix, store, validate) all share the same coupling target: unit-analyze.
